---
title: "Dashboards for Clicker Data"
author: '[[Maryam Albakry (ma878), Rafael Barrios (reb368), Abbie Goldstein (acg229)]]'
subtitle: INFO 4100 Learning Analytics
output:
  word_document: default
---

This project is about developing a learning analytics dashboard based on clicker data. You will work as a team to learn how to make a dashboard using R Shiny (official page with several tutorials: <https://shiny.rstudio.com/tutorial/>).

**Learning Objectives**

1.  Understand the structure of clicker data
2.  Create multiple different visualizations
3.  Design and implement an instructor and student dashboard
4.  Critically evaluate your own dashboard design

You are given aggregated clicker records for a CS course taught at Cornell. There are two datasets: the experience dataset and the quiz dataset.

**Scenario**

You are approached by a college instructor who uses iClickers in her CS class on Business Intelligence. She would like to gain insights about her students and how they are engaging/performing in order to better help them in class. She would also like to better support students by giving them feedback at scale about where they stand and perhaps how they compare to others in the class.

You offer to build a prototype of a dashboard using her clicker data: this is a dashboard for the instructor which offers an overview of the class characteristics, engagement, and performance; and it is a dashboard for students which offers a specific student an overview of their engagement and performance (and how it compares to others).

**Data**

The **experience dataset** contains one record per student who completed the CS course between 2016-2018. There are two sources to this dataset: Faculty Center and a Skills Survey (administered via the Blackboard LMS) where students self reported their skill level for various skills the first week of class. This data has been de-identified. Name, netid, emplid, major have all been removed and replaced with a unique numeric identifier. Note that not all students completed the skills survey, they will have null values for the survey result fields.

| Attribute Name           | Data Type                      | Definition                                                                                                                        |
|--------------------------|--------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| student_key              | numeric Unique key             | Assigned as part of de-identification process. Uniquely identifies student records for this data set only.                        |
| year                     | numeric                        | Four digit year student was enrolled in BI Class.                                                                                 |
| prog                     | character Values (GRAD, UGRAD) | Indicates whether the student was a graduate or undergraduate student when they were enrolled in BI course.                       |
| database_score           | numeric (0-5)                  | Self reported experience level with database technology prior to taking course. 0= no experience, 5= expertise                    |
| sql_score                | numeric (0-5)                  | Self reported experience level with SQL prior to taking course. 0= no experience, 5=expertise                                     |
| programing_score         | numeric (0-5)                  | Self reported experience level with Any Programing language prior to taking course. 0=no experience, 5=expertise                  |
| stored_proc_score        | numeric (0-5)                  | Self reported experience level with stored procedure languages prior to taking course. 0=no experience, 5=expertise               |
| etl_score                | numeric (0-5)                  | Self reported experience level with Extract Transform Load (ETL) development prior to taking course. 0=no experience, 5=expertise |
| data_vis_score           | numeric (0-5)                  | Self reported experience level using data visualization tools prior to taking course. 0=no experience, 5=expertise                |
| requirement_gather_score | numeric (0-5)                  | Self reported experience level gathering customer requirements prior to taking course. 0=no experience, 5=expertise               |
| skill_survey_score       | numeric                        | Sum of the self reported skill level scores.                                                                                      |

The **quiz dataset** contains one record per student per class session held where iClickers were used. Sources used in the creation of this data set include: iClicker session xml files, Blackboard gradebook (for quiz scores), and the Blackboard class schedule (used to map iClicker session to related quiz scores). Note that in some cases there are multiple iClicker sessions / lectures associated with a single quiz. This dataset may be joined to the experience dataset by the student_key field.

| Attribute Name          | Data Type     | Definition                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
|-------------------------|---------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Acad_date_key           | numeric       | Date key in the form of YYYYMMDD indicating the date the class session was held.                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| student_key             | numeric       | Unique identifier for students who took BI class 2016-2018. This key is the primary key for the experience_data file.                                                                                                                                              |
| year                    | numeric       | Four digit year class session was held.                                                                                                                                                                                                                            |
| session_number          | numeric       | Identifies the session number for a particular semester. Session number is assigned by iClicker.                                                                                                                                                                   |
| quiz_number             | numeric       | There are 10 quizzes throughout the BI course. This attribute indicates which quiz is associated with the iClicker session(s).                                                                                                                                     |
| attended                | numeric (0,1) | Binary indicating whether the student attended that particular class session / lecture. 0=no, 1=yes.                                                                                                                                                               |
| total_possible_clicker  | numeric       | The total number of iClicker questions asked that session.                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
| total_completed_clicker | numeric       | The number of iClicker questions answered by student that session.                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| completed_q_clicker     | numeric       | The number of completed Quiz iClicker questions                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| correct_q_clicker       | numeric       | How many correct Quiz answers by student that session.                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| completed_t_clicker     | number        | How many Temperature questions answered by student that session. Temperature questions are 0-5, 0= bad, 5=great. There is no correct answer to Temperature questions, they are used to guage how students are feeling about a particular subject, assignment, etc. |
| avg_t_clicker           | number        | The average temperature answer by student for that session. An average of 1 or 2 would be generally negative, while 4 or 5 would be generally positive responses.                                                                                                  |
| quiz_score              | numeric       | Quiz score out of 20 points possible.                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |

```{r warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)

# install.packages("shiny")
library(shiny)

# install.packages("shinydashboard")
library(shinydashboard)

# This should load the datasets:
# experience (123 by 11) and quiz (2423 by 13)
load("info4100.proj.dashboard.rda")

# Normalize column names to uppercase for consistency
names(quiz)       <- toupper(names(quiz))
names(experience) <- toupper(names(experience))
```

# Part 1: Planning / Sketching

Go through the planning / sketching process described in the reading about dashboards. While some dashboards are certainly better than others, there is not one correct solution here. However, spending enough time to make a concrete plan is essential for the success of your project. Everything you do to make the dashboards will be easier if you have a clear plan, especially because you will be splitting up the work and everyone needs to know what they should work on.

**Question 1:** You will make a student dashboard and a teacher dashboard. Carefully consider the implications of this for design and content. To plan, answer the following prompts once for the student dashboard and then for the teacher dashboard. The more concrete you are here the easier it will be later. Focus on the concrete ideas that you will implement in the next steps. You can iterate on this step and modify your responses as your ideas for the dashboard become clearer. You should explore the dataset in R for 5-10 minutes to get a good sense of what the dataset has to offer.

*Planning for the student dashboard*

-   For whom? Who will use it and what is their background?
    -   Individual students enrolled in the BI course (both graduate and undergraduate), who range from little to moderate technical experience.
    -   Students are familiar with their own quiz grades but may not know how they compare to classmates or how their attendance relates to performance.
-   Why? What is the goal? What questions to answer?
    -   Help each student understand their personal engagement (attendance, clicker participation) and performance (quiz scores) over the semester.
    -   Answer: "How are my quiz scores trending?", "How does my attendance compare to the class?", "How engaged am I relative to my peers?"
-   What? What data to show and what is its structure?
    -   Quiz scores per quiz number (one row per student-session in quiz dataset, joined to experience for skill info).
    -   Attendance rate across sessions, clicker completion rate, average temperature responses, and self-reported skill scores.
-   How? How will visualizations support the goal?
    -   A line chart overlays the selected student's quiz scores against class averages so progress is immediately visible.
    -   Bar charts show attendance and clicker engagement side-by-side with class benchmarks for easy self-assessment.

*Planning for the teacher dashboard*

-   For whom? Who will use it and what is their background?
    -   The course instructor, who is familiar with the course content and comfortable reading quantitative summaries but may not have time for deep data analysis.
    -   The instructor wants a high-level overview with the ability to spot struggling students or session-level problems quickly.
-   Why? What is the goal? What questions to answer?
    -   Provide a class-wide view of attendance, engagement, and performance to support timely instructional decisions.
    -   Answer: "Is attendance dropping?", "Which quiz did students struggle with?", "Do grad vs. undergrad students perform differently?"
-   What? What data to show and what is its structure?
    -   Aggregate quiz scores per quiz number, attendance per session, clicker engagement rates, and skill survey distributions broken out by program (GRAD/UGRAD).
-   How? How will visualizations support the goal?
    -   A boxplot per quiz number reveals score distributions and outliers at a glance.
    -   A line chart of session-level attendance makes declining engagement obvious over time.
    -   A grouped bar chart compares GRAD vs. UGRAD average performance on each quiz.

**Question 2:** Based on your plan above, make a sketch of what the dashboard would look like. See this week's readings for examples. Be detailed about what kinds of data points and visualizations you want to see in different parts of the page. Consider the user experience and how you should position more general information compared to more specific information, and where you may need some additional explanation to help the viewer understand a graphic, for example. In your sketch, it is useful to give labels to different objects, because in the steps below you can split up work between team members and the labels will help you connect the UI with the data objects. Show your sketches in section to get feedback from the teaching team.

Each dashboard should contain at least 4 data visualizations. You may include any additional summary statistics (e.g. key percentages or tables).

*Instructor Dashboard Sketch:*

```
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  [inst.info1] Total Students  |  [inst.info2] Avg Attend |  [inst.info3] Avg Quiz Score
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  [inst.plot1] Quiz Score Distributions (boxplot by quiz) |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  [inst.plot2] Attendance by Session (line over sessions) |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  [inst.plot3] GRAD vs UGRAD Avg Quiz Score (grouped bar) |  [inst.plot4] Skill Survey Distribution (bar)
+----------------------------------------------------------+
```

*Student Dashboard Sketch:*

```
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  [Student selector dropdown]                             |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  [stu.info1] My Avg Quiz Score vs Class  |  [stu.info2] My Attendance Rate
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  [stu.plot1] My Quiz Scores vs Class Avg (line)          |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  [stu.plot2] My Clicker Completion vs Class (bar)        |  [stu.plot3] My Temp Responses (line)
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  [stu.plot4] My Skill Profile (bar)                      |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
```

# Part 2: Dashboard Wire-frame Implementation

This is where you generate the dashboard layout. You are given a very basic wire frame example for the dashboard below. For more information on how R Shiny Dashboards work, look at <https://rstudio.github.io/shinydashboard/get_started.html> and <https://rstudio.github.io/shinydashboard/structure.html>. You can add different types of content into a `fuidRow()`. In the starter code, there are 2 rows of content: the first has two little info boxes; the second has two larger viz boxes. You can add more rows and change what is in them as you wish. Follow the naming convention, e.g. `inst.info1` is the first info box for instructors.

Your team can split up the tasks. Some work on creating the UI (this part), while others work on pre-processing the data and creating the statistics and visualizations that will populate the UI (next part).

**Question 3:** Create the layout for the dashboard tabs. You can have as many "tabs" as you like. Each tab is the content displayed when the user clicks on one of the menu items (so it is the page content). Here you are just specifying the wire frame i.e. **what goes where on the pages**, not what goes into it.

```{r}
#######################################
####### BEGIN INPUT: Question 3 #######
#######################################

instructor_dash = tabItem(
    tabName = "instructor",
    h2("Instructor Dashboard"),

    # Row 1: summary info boxes
    fluidRow(
      infoBoxOutput("inst.info1"),
      infoBoxOutput("inst.info2"),
      infoBoxOutput("inst.info3")
    ),

    # Row 2: quiz score distributions across quizzes
    fluidRow(
      box(
        title = "Quiz Score Distribution by Quiz Number",
        width = 12,
        plotOutput("inst.plot1", height = 300)
      )
    ),

    # Row 3: attendance trend over sessions
    fluidRow(
      box(
        title = "Class Attendance Rate by Session",
        width = 12,
        plotOutput("inst.plot2", height = 280)
      )
    ),

    # Row 4: GRAD vs UGRAD performance + skill survey
    fluidRow(
      box(
        title = "Average Quiz Score: GRAD vs. UGRAD",
        width = 6,
        plotOutput("inst.plot3", height = 280)
      ),
      box(
        title = "Skill Survey Score Distribution",
        width = 6,
        plotOutput("inst.plot4", height = 280)
      )
    )
)

student_dash = tabItem(
  tabName = "student",
  h2("Student Dashboard"),

  # Student selector
  fluidRow(
    box(
      width = 4,
      selectInput("student_id", "Select Student ID:", choices = NULL)
    )
  ),

  # Row 1: personal summary info boxes
  fluidRow(
    infoBoxOutput("stu.info1"),
    infoBoxOutput("stu.info2")
  ),

  # Row 2: quiz score trend vs class
  fluidRow(
    box(
      title = "My Quiz Scores vs. Class Average",
      width = 12,
      plotOutput("stu.plot1", height = 300)
    )
  ),

  # Row 3: clicker completion + temperature engagement
  fluidRow(
    box(
      title = "Clicker Completion Rate vs. Class Average",
      width = 6,
      plotOutput("stu.plot2", height = 280)
    ),
    box(
      title = "My Temperature (Engagement) Responses Over Sessions",
      width = 6,
      plotOutput("stu.plot3", height = 280)
    )
  ),

  # Row 4: skill profile
  fluidRow(
    box(
      title = "My Skill Profile (Self-Reported)",
      width = 12,
      plotOutput("stu.plot4", height = 280)
    )
  )
)

#######################################
#######################################
```

# Part 3: Data Pre-processing

Get the data ready for use in the dashboard. Before the next stage, you want to have the data ready in the right format for simple computations and plotting. To do this effectively, you need to know by now what you want to display in each dashboard. However, this is also an iterative process. Once you have completed a first iteration of the design, you can come back to this step and add further pre-processing for more visualizations you like to add. This step is also an opportunity to better understand the structure of the datasets.

The instructor dashboard should show information for all students. The student dashboard is typically focused on an individual student. You can either pick a student (at random or intentionally) and use them as the "reference student" for the student dashboard. Or, a bit more ambitious but also more rewarding to try out, you can create an interactive dashboard in which you select the student and then the dashboard updates to show the information for that student. I would recommend you start with the simpler version and get that to work before you try to make it dynamic.

Use the space below to be ready for your information visualizations in the dashboards.

```{r}
#######################################
####### BEGIN INPUT             #######
#######################################

# ── Instructor-level aggregations ──────────────────────────────────────────

# Average class attendance rate per session (for line chart)
session_attendance <- quiz %>%
  group_by(SESSION_NUMBER) %>%
  summarise(attend_rate = mean(ATTENDED, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# Quiz score distributions — one row per student-quiz (take first occurrence per student/quiz)
quiz_scores_by_quiz <- quiz %>%
  filter(!is.na(QUIZ_SCORE)) %>%
  distinct(STUDENT_KEY, QUIZ_NUMBER, .keep_all = TRUE)

# Average quiz score by GRAD/UGRAD per quiz
grad_ugrad_scores <- quiz %>%
  filter(!is.na(QUIZ_SCORE)) %>%
  distinct(STUDENT_KEY, QUIZ_NUMBER, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  left_join(experience %>% select(STUDENT_KEY, PROG), by = "STUDENT_KEY") %>%
  filter(!is.na(PROG)) %>%
  group_by(QUIZ_NUMBER, PROG) %>%
  summarise(avg_score = mean(QUIZ_SCORE, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# Skill survey score distribution (one row per student)
skill_dist <- experience %>%
  select(STUDENT_KEY, SKILL_SURVEY_SCORE) %>%
  filter(!is.na(SKILL_SURVEY_SCORE))

# ── Class-level benchmarks for student dashboard ───────────────────────────

# Class average quiz score per quiz number
class_avg_quiz <- quiz %>%
  filter(!is.na(QUIZ_SCORE)) %>%
  distinct(STUDENT_KEY, QUIZ_NUMBER, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  group_by(QUIZ_NUMBER) %>%
  summarise(class_avg = mean(QUIZ_SCORE, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# Class average clicker completion rate per session
class_avg_clicker <- quiz %>%
  mutate(completion_rate = ifelse(TOTAL_POSSIBLE_CLICKER > 0,
                                  TOTAL_COMPLETED_CLICKER / TOTAL_POSSIBLE_CLICKER,
                                  NA_real_)) %>%
  group_by(SESSION_NUMBER) %>%
  summarise(class_completion = mean(completion_rate, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# Class average skill scores (for comparison in student profile)
skill_cols <- c("DATABASE_SCORE","SQL_SCORE","PROGRAMING_SCORE",
                "STORED_PROC_SCORE","ETL_SCORE","DATA_VIS_SCORE","REQUIREMENT_GATHER_SCORE")

class_avg_skills <- experience %>%
  summarise(across(all_of(skill_cols), ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE))) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "skill", values_to = "class_avg") %>%
  mutate(skill = recode(skill,
    DATABASE_SCORE = "Database",
    SQL_SCORE = "SQL",
    PROGRAMING_SCORE = "Programming",
    STORED_PROC_SCORE = "Stored Proc",
    ETL_SCORE = "ETL",
    DATA_VIS_SCORE = "Data Vis",
    REQUIREMENT_GATHER_SCORE = "Req. Gathering"
  ))

#######################################
#######################################
```

# Part 4: Prepare All Data Visualizations

This is where you create the content for the wire frames you created above. Again, you can refer to the examples and documentation in <https://rstudio.github.io/shinydashboard/get_started.html> and <https://rstudio.github.io/shinydashboard/structure.html> for guidance. You can also find many examples online just by searching with Google.

**Question 4:** For each of the pieces of content you planned for in the wire frames above, generate the relevant content. You need to assign them all to the `output` variable by referencing the name of the wire frame element you chose above like this `output$name.of.element`.

```{r}
server = function(input, output, session) {

#######################################
####### BEGIN INPUT: Question 4 #######
#######################################

  # Populate student selector with all unique student IDs
  observe({
    ids <- sort(unique(quiz$STUDENT_KEY))
    updateSelectInput(session, "student_id", choices = ids, selected = ids[1])
  })

  # ── INSTRUCTOR INFO BOXES ──────────────────────────────────────────────────

  output$inst.info1 = renderInfoBox({
    infoBox("Total Students",
            length(unique(quiz$STUDENT_KEY)),
            icon = icon("users"), color = "purple")
  })

  output$inst.info2 = renderInfoBox({
    infoBox("Avg Attendance",
            paste0(round(100 * mean(quiz$ATTENDED, na.rm = TRUE)), "%"),
            icon = icon("calendar-check"), color = "blue")
  })

  output$inst.info3 = renderInfoBox({
    avg_score <- quiz %>%
      filter(!is.na(QUIZ_SCORE)) %>%
      distinct(STUDENT_KEY, QUIZ_NUMBER, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
      summarise(m = mean(QUIZ_SCORE, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
      pull(m)
    infoBox("Avg Quiz Score",
            paste0(round(avg_score, 1), " / 20"),
            icon = icon("graduation-cap"), color = "green")
  })

  # ── INSTRUCTOR PLOTS ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  # inst.plot1: Boxplot of quiz scores by quiz number
  output$inst.plot1 = renderPlot({
    ggplot(quiz_scores_by_quiz, aes(x = factor(QUIZ_NUMBER), y = QUIZ_SCORE)) +
      geom_boxplot(fill = "#7B68EE", alpha = 0.7, outlier.colour = "red", outlier.alpha = 0.5) +
      stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", shape = 18, size = 3, color = "white") +
      labs(x = "Quiz Number", y = "Score (out of 20)",
           caption = "White diamond = class mean; red points = outliers") +
      theme_classic() +
      theme(axis.text = element_text(size = 12),
            axis.title = element_text(size = 13))
  })

  # inst.plot2: Attendance rate per session (line chart)
  output$inst.plot2 = renderPlot({
    ggplot(session_attendance, aes(x = SESSION_NUMBER, y = attend_rate)) +
      geom_line(color = "#4682B4", linewidth = 1.2) +
      geom_point(color = "#4682B4", size = 2.5) +
      geom_hline(yintercept = mean(session_attendance$attend_rate),
                 linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
      scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1), limits = c(0, 1)) +
      labs(x = "Session Number", y = "Attendance Rate",
           caption = "Dashed line = overall average") +
      theme_classic() +
      theme(axis.text = element_text(size = 12),
            axis.title = element_text(size = 13))
  })

  # inst.plot3: Grouped bar — GRAD vs UGRAD avg quiz score per quiz
  output$inst.plot3 = renderPlot({
    ggplot(grad_ugrad_scores, aes(x = factor(QUIZ_NUMBER), y = avg_score, fill = PROG)) +
      geom_col(position = "dodge", alpha = 0.85) +
      scale_fill_manual(values = c(GRAD = "#E87722", UGRAD = "#006EB6"),
                        name = "Program") +
      labs(x = "Quiz Number", y = "Average Score (out of 20)") +
      theme_classic() +
      theme(axis.text = element_text(size = 11),
            axis.title = element_text(size = 12),
            legend.position = "bottom")
  })

  # inst.plot4: Skill survey score distribution (histogram)
  output$inst.plot4 = renderPlot({
    ggplot(skill_dist, aes(x = SKILL_SURVEY_SCORE)) +
      geom_histogram(binwidth = 2, fill = "#6AAC3A", color = "white", alpha = 0.85) +
      geom_vline(xintercept = mean(skill_dist$SKILL_SURVEY_SCORE, na.rm = TRUE),
                 linetype = "dashed", color = "darkgreen", linewidth = 1) +
      labs(x = "Skill Survey Total Score", y = "Number of Students",
           caption = "Dashed line = class mean") +
      theme_classic() +
      theme(axis.text = element_text(size = 11),
            axis.title = element_text(size = 12))
  })

  # ── STUDENT REACTIVE DATA ─────────────────────────────────────────────────

  stu_quiz <- reactive({
    req(input$student_id)
    quiz %>%
      filter(STUDENT_KEY == as.numeric(input$student_id),
             !is.na(QUIZ_SCORE)) %>%
      distinct(QUIZ_NUMBER, .keep_all = TRUE)
  })

  stu_sessions <- reactive({
    req(input$student_id)
    quiz %>%
      filter(STUDENT_KEY == as.numeric(input$student_id))
  })

  stu_exp <- reactive({
    req(input$student_id)
    experience %>%
      filter(STUDENT_KEY == as.numeric(input$student_id))
  })

  # ── STUDENT INFO BOXES ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  output$stu.info1 = renderInfoBox({
    my_avg  <- mean(stu_quiz()$QUIZ_SCORE, na.rm = TRUE)
    cl_avg  <- mean(quiz$QUIZ_SCORE, na.rm = TRUE)
    delta   <- round(my_avg - cl_avg, 1)
    sign_str <- ifelse(delta >= 0, paste0("+", delta), as.character(delta))
    infoBox("My Avg Quiz Score",
            paste0(round(my_avg, 1), " / 20  (", sign_str, " vs class)"),
            icon = icon("star"), color = ifelse(delta >= 0, "green", "red"))
  })

  output$stu.info2 = renderInfoBox({
    my_att  <- mean(stu_sessions()$ATTENDED, na.rm = TRUE)
    cl_att  <- mean(quiz$ATTENDED, na.rm = TRUE)
    delta   <- round((my_att - cl_att) * 100, 1)
    sign_str <- ifelse(delta >= 0, paste0("+", delta, "%"), paste0(delta, "%"))
    infoBox("My Attendance Rate",
            paste0(round(my_att * 100), "%  (", sign_str, " vs class)"),
            icon = icon("calendar"), color = ifelse(delta >= 0, "green", "yellow"))
  })

  # ── STUDENT PLOTS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  # stu.plot1: Student quiz scores vs class average per quiz (line)
  output$stu.plot1 = renderPlot({
    stu_data <- stu_quiz() %>%
      select(QUIZ_NUMBER, QUIZ_SCORE) %>%
      rename(my_score = QUIZ_SCORE)

    plot_data <- class_avg_quiz %>%
      left_join(stu_data, by = "QUIZ_NUMBER")

    ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = QUIZ_NUMBER)) +
      geom_line(aes(y = class_avg, color = "Class Average"), linewidth = 1.1, linetype = "dashed") +
      geom_line(aes(y = my_score, color = "My Score"), linewidth = 1.3, na.rm = TRUE) +
      geom_point(aes(y = my_score, color = "My Score"), size = 3, na.rm = TRUE) +
      geom_point(aes(y = class_avg, color = "Class Average"), size = 2.5) +
      scale_color_manual(values = c("My Score" = "#E87722", "Class Average" = "#4682B4"),
                         name = NULL) +
      scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:10) +
      labs(x = "Quiz Number", y = "Score (out of 20)") +
      theme_classic() +
      theme(axis.text = element_text(size = 12),
            axis.title = element_text(size = 13),
            legend.position = "bottom")
  })

  # stu.plot2: Clicker completion rate per session vs class avg (bar + line)
  output$stu.plot2 = renderPlot({
    stu_comp <- stu_sessions() %>%
      mutate(completion_rate = ifelse(TOTAL_POSSIBLE_CLICKER > 0,
                                      TOTAL_COMPLETED_CLICKER / TOTAL_POSSIBLE_CLICKER,
                                      NA_real_)) %>%
      select(SESSION_NUMBER, completion_rate) %>%
      left_join(class_avg_clicker, by = "SESSION_NUMBER")

    ggplot(stu_comp, aes(x = SESSION_NUMBER)) +
      geom_col(aes(y = completion_rate, fill = "My Rate"), alpha = 0.75, na.rm = TRUE) +
      geom_line(aes(y = class_completion, color = "Class Avg"),
                linewidth = 1.2, linetype = "dashed") +
      scale_fill_manual(values = c("My Rate" = "#E87722"), name = NULL) +
      scale_color_manual(values = c("Class Avg" = "#4682B4"), name = NULL) +
      scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1), limits = c(0, 1.05)) +
      labs(x = "Session Number", y = "Clicker Completion Rate") +
      theme_classic() +
      theme(axis.text = element_text(size = 11),
            axis.title = element_text(size = 12),
            legend.position = "bottom")
  })

  # stu.plot3: Student temperature responses over sessions
  output$stu.plot3 = renderPlot({
    temp_data <- stu_sessions() %>%
      filter(!is.na(AVG_T_CLICKER), COMPLETED_T_CLICKER > 0)

    if (nrow(temp_data) == 0) {
      return(
        ggplot() +
          annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = 0.5, label = "No temperature data for this student.",
                   size = 5) +
          theme_void()
      )
    }

    ggplot(temp_data, aes(x = SESSION_NUMBER, y = AVG_T_CLICKER)) +
      geom_line(color = "#9B59B6", linewidth = 1.2) +
      geom_point(color = "#9B59B6", size = 3) +
      geom_hline(yintercept = 3, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
      scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 5), breaks = 0:5) +
      labs(x = "Session Number", y = "Avg Temperature Response (0–5)",
           caption = "Dashed line = neutral midpoint (3)") +
      theme_classic() +
      theme(axis.text = element_text(size = 11),
            axis.title = element_text(size = 12))
  })

  # stu.plot4: Student skill profile vs class average (grouped bar)
  output$stu.plot4 = renderPlot({
    exp_row <- stu_exp()

    if (nrow(exp_row) == 0 || all(is.na(exp_row[, skill_cols]))) {
      return(
        ggplot() +
          annotate("text", x = 0.5, y = 0.5,
                   label = "No skill survey data for this student.", size = 5) +
          theme_void()
      )
    }

    stu_skills <- exp_row %>%
      select(all_of(skill_cols)) %>%
      pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "skill", values_to = "my_score") %>%
      mutate(skill = recode(skill,
        DATABASE_SCORE = "Database",
        SQL_SCORE = "SQL",
        PROGRAMING_SCORE = "Programming",
        STORED_PROC_SCORE = "Stored Proc",
        ETL_SCORE = "ETL",
        DATA_VIS_SCORE = "Data Vis",
        REQUIREMENT_GATHER_SCORE = "Req. Gathering"
      )) %>%
      left_join(class_avg_skills, by = "skill") %>%
      pivot_longer(c(my_score, class_avg), names_to = "who", values_to = "score") %>%
      mutate(who = recode(who, my_score = "Me", class_avg = "Class Avg"))

    ggplot(stu_skills, aes(x = skill, y = score, fill = who)) +
      geom_col(position = "dodge", alpha = 0.85, na.rm = TRUE) +
      scale_fill_manual(values = c("Me" = "#E87722", "Class Avg" = "#4682B4"), name = NULL) +
      scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 5), breaks = 0:5) +
      labs(x = "Skill", y = "Self-Reported Score (0–5)") +
      theme_classic() +
      theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1, size = 11),
            axis.title = element_text(size = 12),
            legend.position = "bottom")
  })

#######################################
#######################################

}
```

# Part 5: Produce Dashboard and Reflect

You should be able to simply run the code below **as is** to see your dashboard.

**Note:** Unfortunately, you cannot knit this part into a pdf. So I added `eval=FALSE` to let the knitting run smoothly and you can submit your PDF.

```{r eval=FALSE}
#######################################
### This code creates the dashboard ###
#######################################

dhead = dashboardHeader(title = "Clicker Dashboard")

dside = dashboardSidebar(
  sidebarMenu(
    menuItem("Instructor View", tabName = "instructor", icon = icon("dashboard")),
    menuItem("Student View",    tabName = "student",    icon = icon("th"))
  )
)

dbody = dashboardBody(
    tabItems(
      instructor_dash,
      student_dash
    )
)

ui = dashboardPage(dhead, dside, dbody)

shinyApp(ui, server)
```

**Question 5:** Add screenshots of your group's dashboards below using this syntax or simply add them to the Word document after knitting:

![Dashboard Screenshot](example_dash.png)

**Question 6:** Evaluate your group dashboard from the perspective of the instructor (teacher dashboard) and from the perspective of the student (student dashboard). What do you like about it, what would you change or add to it if you had more time?

*Reflection for the student dashboard*

-   What do you like about it?
    -   We like the direct personal-vs-class comparison built into every panel. The info boxes at the top give an instant "+/-" signal so a student can gauge their standing without reading the charts at all. The quiz score line chart makes it easy to see whether performance is improving or declining over time, and the skill profile bar chart puts the student's self-assessment in context against their peers.
-   What would you change or add to it if you had more time?
    -   We would add a "predicted final grade" estimate based on the regression of attendance and quiz scores. We would also add a heatmap of which specific quiz sessions a student missed, and allow the student to filter by year so they could see whether they are keeping pace with prior cohorts.
-   What was the biggest challenge you faced? How did you address it?
    -   The biggest challenge was handling students who had missing skill survey data or no temperature responses. We addressed this with explicit `nrow == 0` / `all(is.na())` guards that render a friendly message rather than crashing the plot.

*Reflection for the teacher dashboard*

-   What do you like about it?
    -   The boxplot of quiz score distributions gives an immediate picture of where the class struggled and where there was high variability. The GRAD vs. UGRAD comparison bar chart answers a question instructors often have intuitively but rarely have data to answer directly. The attendance line chart makes session-level drop-offs visible at a glance, which could prompt the instructor to investigate what happened on a particular day.
-   What would you change or add to it if you had more time?
    -   We would add an individual-student drill-down table so the instructor could click a quiz number in the boxplot and see which students scored below a threshold. A correlation plot between skill survey scores and final quiz averages would also help the instructor understand whether prior background predicts success.
-   What was the biggest challenge you faced? How did you address it?
    -   Handling duplicate student-quiz rows (multiple iClicker sessions per quiz) was tricky. We addressed it by using `distinct(STUDENT_KEY, QUIZ_NUMBER, .keep_all = TRUE)` so each student contributes one score per quiz to the distribution, matching the conceptual unit the instructor cares about.

# Estimate time spent

**We want to give students an estimate of how much time this homework will take. Please indicate how many hours you spent to complete this homework here.**

-   I spent approximately 8 hours across team members completing this project.

# Generative AI usage

**As stated in the course syllabus, using generative AI is allowed to help you as you complete this homework. We are interested in how it is being used and whether it is helpful for you.**

-   We used generative AI (Claude) for most of the coding portions, particularly in scaffolding the Shiny reactive logic and ggplot2 syntax.
-   It was helpful for quickly generating boilerplate Shiny server/UI code and suggesting appropriate plot types for each data question. We still needed to tailor the variable names to match the actual dataset columns and verify that the reactive logic behaved correctly.

# Submit Project

This is the end of the group project. Please **Knit to Word** (if you run into an issue, you can knit to PDF). The resulting file has to show both the R code and R output. Upload it on Canvas before the due date.
